1: A type: “Son of man, stand on your feet, and I will speak with you.” The divine command to stand after prostration is a resurrection pattern: the prophet who falls before the divine glory is raised to stand and receive mission. Christ himself falls prostrate in Gethsemane (“he fell on his face,” Matthew 26:39) and rises from death to stand as the exalted Lord. The “son of man” address — used 93 times in Ezekiel — is the title Jesus adopts for himself, linking his self-understanding to Ezekiel’s commissioned humanity standing before the divine presence.
2: A type: “As he spoke to me, the Spirit entered into me and set me on my feet.” The Spirit’s entry enabling the prophet to stand is the prophetic pattern of Spirit-empowered mission. Jesus is described as Spirit-empowered throughout his ministry (Luke 4:1, 14, 18), and the resurrection itself is attributed to the Spirit (“the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead,” Romans 8:11). The Spirit who sets Ezekiel on his feet is the same Spirit who raises Christ from the dead and sets the church on its feet for mission (Acts 1:8).
3: A pattern: “I am sending you to the Israelites — to nations that have rebelled against me.” The prophetic commission to rebellious Israel is the OT pattern of Jesus’s own mission: “I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel” (Matthew 15:24); “he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him” (John 1:11). Ezekiel’s commission to a persistently rebellious audience defines the prophetic task that Jesus takes on in his full scope — sent to the most resistant recipients.
4: A pattern: “They are brazen-faced and stubborn-hearted... you shall say: Thus says the Lord GOD.” The prophetic word is commanded regardless of the audience’s condition. Jesus in John’s Gospel speaks his words whether they are received or rejected: “the words that I have spoken to you are spirit and life” (John 6:63), yet many of his disciples turned back. The pattern of proclamation over resistance connects Ezekiel’s commission to Jesus’s ministry and then to the apostolic mission pattern of Acts.
5: A pattern: “Whether they listen or refuse — for they are a rebellious house — they will know that a prophet has been among them.” The ultimate vindication of the prophet’s words comes through historical fulfillment rather than immediate reception. Jesus’s prediction of Jerusalem’s fall (Luke 21:20-24) was fulfilled in 70 CE, demonstrating after the fact that a prophet had been among them. The rejected word of Christ is eventually recognized even by those who rejected it — at the final judgment or in the retrospective of history.
6: A pattern: “Do not be afraid of them, or be terrified by their looks, though they are a rebellious house.” The anti-fear commission is given to Ezekiel because prophetic mission faces genuine hostility. Jesus commissions his disciples with similar anti-fear language (“do not fear those who kill the body,” Matthew 10:28) and models it himself — he did not recoil from his opponents’ “looks” even when they picked up stones to throw at him (John 10:31-39).
7: A pattern: “You shall speak my words to them, whether they listen or not.” The unconditional quality of the prophetic commission — outcome-independent fidelity to the message — is the pattern of Jesus’s own ministry and the apostolic mission. Paul echoes this in 2 Timothy 4:2: “preach the word; be ready in season and out of season.” The prophet who speaks only when reception seems likely has already compromised the message.
8: A type: “Do not be like that rebellious house. Open your mouth and eat what I give you.” The eating of the divine word is the type of receiving God’s revelation as nourishment. Jesus applies this to himself: “I have food to eat that you do not know about... my food is to do the will of him who sent me” (John 4:32-34). The divine word is not merely information to transmit but substance to be internalized — the prophet, like Christ, is consumed by the word before he speaks it.
9: A type: “A hand stretched out toward me, and in it was a written scroll.” The scroll given to Ezekiel by the divine hand is the OT archetype of the sealed scroll of Rev 5, which only the Lamb can open. The divine word comes to the prophet through the hand of YHWH — the same hand that, in the incarnation, stretched out on the cross. John the Apostle receives a similar scroll from the divine hand in Revelation 10:8-10, consciously re-enacting Ezekiel’s commissioning.
10: A type: “He unrolled it before me — it had writing on both sides — and on it were lamentations, mourning, and woe.” The scroll written on both sides filled with lament is the antithesis of the empty scroll — it is full of divine judgment against sin. The scroll of Rev 5 that only the Lamb can open is also described as written on the front and back (Revelation 5:1), and when it is opened, it releases judgment into the world. Only Christ, having absorbed lamentations, mourning, and woe in his own person on the cross, has the right and capacity to open the scroll of divine judgment.
